title: The Faithful Parent 4: Parenting the Preschooler
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Faithful Parent 4 - Parenting the Preschooler - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: Parenting the preschooler (ages 4-5): same as toddler but deeper
Modeling and coaching right behavior and responses
Parents as filters for what children are exposed to
A Christian philosophy of education: Christ must have preeminence in all things
Deuteronomy 6:4-9: the pervasiveness and purposefulness of instruction
Loving God with all our minds (Matthew 22:37) applied to education
Bringing every thought captive to Christ (2 Corinthians 10:5)
Answering objections: difficult cases, experience, social adjustment, salt and light
Total involvement in education under the umbrella of Christ
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro continues the Faithful Parent series by addressing the preschool years (ages 4-5), emphasizing that parenting at this stage is the same as with toddlers but at a deeper level of instruction. The bulk of the lesson develops a Christian philosophy of education, arguing from Deuteronomy 6, Ephesians 6:4, Matthew 22:37, and 2 Corinthians 10:5 that Christ must have preeminence in all education — not just religious subjects but every academic discipline. Common objections to distinctly Christian schooling are examined and answered.
transcript:
## Parenting the Preschooler (Ages 4–5)

We are continuing our series on the Faithful Parent. The goal of life is to enjoy and glorify God — every one of us is placed on this earth for that purpose. To enjoy and glorify God is to do what is right according to his word. It is not necessarily an emotional state or a warm feeling. It is obedience. And that is how we enjoy and glorify God in parenting as well.

We have considered parenting the infant and parenting the toddler. Today we move into the preschool years — four and five years old. The simple lesson for this stage: **parenting the preschooler is the same as parenting the toddler, but deeper.**

The preschooler is better able to understand God's word. You can go more deeply into Scripture with them. Paul commended Timothy, knowing from childhood he had been taught the Holy Scriptures, "which are able to make you wise for salvation through faith which is in Christ Jesus" (2 Timothy 3:15).

Children at this age still take God at face value — they have not yet grown cynical. The Lord Jesus said that unless we have the faith of a little child, we will not enter the kingdom of heaven. That kind of simple trust is a grace.

## Modeling and Revisiting Instructions

At this stage, modeling becomes increasingly important. When your four or five year old does not graciously obey, you must continue to train him. If he does not honor you with his tone of voice, the way he looks at you, or his words when responding to a command, put the original instruction aside and coach him to restate the right words in the right tone. Model the correct response. Then return to the original instruction.

If you deem that spanking is appropriate, do that — and then after spanking, revisit the original instruction. Go back and say, "Now go ahead and put your toys away," and if he does not do it, the pattern repeats until he obeys correctly.

## Filtering What Children Are Exposed To

During these early years, children are taught by what they are exposed to. Just because something carries a children's rating does not mean it is appropriate. Parents must be the filter for little eyes and hearts. As difficult as it has become with devices and screens, parents must exercise careful oversight over what their children see and hear. Even when the content on a screen is good, constant exposure to any screen is not healthy.

## A Christian Philosophy of Education

As we move toward the school years, we must establish what our philosophy of education is. The order of the words matters: not a "philosophy of Christian education" but a **Christian philosophy of all education** — that is, a Christian worldview governing every academic subject.

What is the place of Christ in all matters? Paul writes:

> *"For of him and through him and to him are all things, to whom be glory forever."* (Romans 11:36)

> *"And he put all things under his feet and gave him to be head over all things to the church."* (Ephesians 1:22)

> *"For by him all things were created that are in heaven and that are on earth... all things were created through him and for him."* (Colossians 1:16)

> *"That in all things he may have the preeminence."* (Colossians 1:18)

Christ has the preeminence in all things — and "all things" includes education. Since even the simplest matters of life are to be done to the glory of God, and since Christ has the preeminence in all things, Christ must have the preeminence in education — not only in religious education, but in every academic subject.

### Deuteronomy 6 and the Pervasiveness of Instruction

> *"Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one! You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your strength. And these words which I command you today shall be in your heart. You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house, when you walk by the way, when you lie down, and when you rise up. You shall bind them as a sign on your hand, and they shall be as frontlets between your eyes. You shall write them on the doorposts of your house and on your gates."* (Deuteronomy 6:4–9)

Notice the pervasiveness: sitting, walking, lying down, rising up — on the hand, the forehead, the door. This does not mean a phylactery strapped to the head. It means the word of God is to control the mind, the hands, and the feet — everywhere, at all times. There is no moment when the word of God is not present in the training of children.

And notice the breadth of what Deuteronomy instructs. As you work through the book, the statutes and judgments cover not only worship but also diet and health (ch. 14), debt management (ch. 15), criminal law (ch. 16–17), civil government (ch. 17), real estate law (ch. 19), principles governing warfare (ch. 20), accounting and finances (ch. 23), engineering (ch. 22), and agronomy (ch. 22). The word of God governs all of life.

### The Great Commandment and Education

> *"You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind."* (Matthew 22:37)

God designed us to love him with all our minds. It follows that our thinking must be based on his word — in history, in math, in science. If we are not teaching our children to think about every academic subject in Christian terms, we are not teaching them to love God with their minds. We are disobeying the commandment Christ himself called the greatest.

People say there is no difference between teaching math from a Christian perspective and from a secular perspective — because two plus two equals four no matter what you believe. But why does two plus two equal four? Because God is a God of order who created a universe that can be counted. There is a foundational difference. If we or our children are not being taught to think about all academic subjects in Christian terms, we are not being taught to love God with all our minds.

### Bringing Every Thought Captive

> *"Casting down arguments and every high thing that exalts itself against the knowledge of God, bringing every thought into captivity to the obedience of Christ."* (2 Corinthians 10:5)

One of the ways we defeat Satan is by bringing every thought captive to Christ. But we cannot bring every thought captive by allowing some thoughts to be autonomous — not captive. We cannot bring all thoughts captive to the obedience of Christ if the only times we think Christianly are during Sunday school and family devotions.

## Answering Objections

**"I know someone who went to secular schools and survived."** Is survival the best we want for our children? And can we be guided by individual experiences when good and bad experiences can be found on every side? The principles of the word of God must guide us, not anecdote.

**"My child can be salt and light in a secular school."** It sounds spiritual. But we would never think of sending our children to a Mormon VBS to be salt and light, or to India alone to be missionaries. Why are we willing to send them to a secular school for six hours a day, 180 days a year? The general characteristic of children is that they are "tossed to and fro and carried about with every wind of doctrine, by the trickery of men, in the cunning craftiness of deceitful plotting" (Ephesians 4:14). These are the people we think can hold the line in an environment designed to push them away from Christ.

**"I can't afford Christian school."** The more honest question is often: Am I unwilling to restructure my priorities? Education for our children should rank near the top of the family budget — after giving, perhaps after the mortgage, but before vacation, new cars, and discretionary spending.

## The Parents' Responsibility

No matter who does the direct teaching — a Christian school, a homeschool co-op, a public school — it is the parents who are accountable before God for the education of their children. Ephesians 6:4 says "fathers... bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord." No parent will stand before God and say, "But I wasn't the one who taught him that."

The marvelous responsibility of parents with regard to education includes:
- Providing a godly environment for instruction
- Teaching children to obey the first and great commandment by loving God with their minds
- Evaluating educational options by the standard of the word of God
- Recognizing the impact that an anti-Christian educational environment will have on their children

This is what it means to be a faithful parent in raising children for the glory of God.
